## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Storage Capacity for a Distributed Big Data Cluster

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the total usable storage capacity of a Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) cluster, accounting for the replication factor.

**Given:**
- A cluster has $N_{nodes}$ data nodes.
- Each data node has a storage capacity of $C_{node}$ terabytes.
- The HDFS replication factor is $RF$.

**Step 1: Total Raw Capacity**
The aggregate raw storage (sum of all node capacities) is computed as the product of the number of nodes and per-node capacity:

$$
C_{raw} = N_{nodes} \times C_{node}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Replication Overhead**
The replication factor $RF$ means each data block is stored $RF$ times for fault tolerance. The *effective* usable capacity is therefore divided by $RF$:

$$
C_{usable} = \frac{C_{raw}}{RF} = \frac{N_{nodes} \times C_{node}}{RF}
$$

**Step 3: Account for HDFS Reserved Space**
HDFS reserves a fraction $f_{reserved}$ (typically 10–15%) of each node for non-HDFS operations (e.g., logs, OS). The final safe capacity is:

$$
C_{safe} = C_{usable} \times (1 - f_{reserved})
$$

**Step 4: Final Compact Form**

$$
C_{safe} = \frac{N_{nodes} \times C_{node} \times (1 - f_{reserved})}{RF}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** For a cluster of $N_{nodes} = 100$ nodes, $C_{node} = 10$ TB, $RF = 3$, and $f_{reserved} = 0.10$, the safe capacity computes to $\approx 300$ TB. This is the **true usable Big Data storage** for analytics.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Big Data vs. Traditional Data Processing

| Analytical Dimension | Traditional RDBMS | Big Data Ecosystem (Hadoop/Spark) |
|---|---|---|
| Data Volume | GB scale | **PB to ZB scale** |
| Data Variety | Structured only | **Structured + Semi + Unstructured** |
| Processing Mode | Batch | **Batch + Real-time stream** |
| Architecture | Centralized (vertical scaling) | **Distributed (horizontal scaling)** |
| Transaction Property | ACID | **BASE (Eventual Consistency)** |
| Schema | Schema-on-write | **Schema-on-read** |
| Hardware Cost | High-end proprietary servers | **Commodity clusters** |
| Fault Tolerance | Limited (RAID) | **Native replication + rebalancing** |
| Query Language | SQL | **SQL-on-Hadoop (HiveQL), Spark SQL** |
| Cost per TB | High (~\$1000s) | **Low (~\$10s, commodity hardware)** |
| Use Case Examples | Banking OLTP, ERP | **IoT analytics, social media mining, smart factories** |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step — Identifying the 5 V's in a Real Industry 4.0 Use Case

> **Scenario:** A smart factory deploys 5,000 IoT sensors on a CNC machining line. Each sensor emits readings (temperature, vibration, pressure) every 10 ms. Data is streamed to a central analytics platform for predictive maintenance.

**Step 1: Identify Volume**
Each sensor generates $\frac{1}{0.01} = 100$ readings/second. Across 5,000 sensors:

$$
R_{total} = 5000 \times 100 = 500{,}000 \text{ records/sec}
$$

Assuming 256 bytes/record, daily volume:

$$
V_{day} = 500{,}000 \times 256 \times 86400 \approx 11.06 \text{ TB/day}
$$

This satisfies the **Volume** V.

**Step 2: Identify Velocity**
Readings arrive every 10 ms, demanding **real-time ingestion** via tools like **Apache Kafka / Apache Flink**. The 100 Hz sampling rate is the **Velocity** V.

**Step 3: Identify Variety**
Sensor data (structured numeric) + maintenance logs (text/semi-structured) + camera images of tool wear (unstructured) → **Variety** V.

**Step 4: Identify Veracity**
Sensor calibration drift, electrical noise, packet loss introduce **uncertainty**. Veracity-aware filtering (e.g., Kalman filters, anomaly detection) is needed → **Veracity** V.

**Step 5: Identify Value**
The analytical output is **predictive maintenance alerts** that reduce unplanned downtime by 30–50% (industry benchmark). This actionable insight is the **Value** V.

> **Conclusion:** This use case exhibits **all 5 V's of Big Data** and is a textbook Industry 4.0 application.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Throughput Calculation

**Problem:** A data center ingests data from 3 sources: (1) 2,000 sensors at 50 Hz each, (2) 100 surveillance cameras streaming 5 Mbps each, and (3) a transactional database at 200 MB/s. Calculate the aggregate ingress throughput.

**Step 1: Sensor throughput**

$$
T_{sensors} = 2000 \times 50 \times 256 \text{ bytes} = 25.6 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 2: Camera throughput**

$$
T_{cameras} = 100 \times 5 \text{ Mbps} = 500 \text{ Mbps} = 62.5 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 3: Transactional throughput**

$$
T_{db} = 200 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 4: Aggregate throughput**

$$
T_{agg} = T_{sensors} + T_{cameras} + T_{db}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
T_{agg} = 25.6 + 62.5 + 200 = 288.1 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to GB/hour for capacity planning**

$$
T_{hour} = 288.1 \times 3600 \approx 1037.16 \text{ GB/hour} \approx 1.04 \text{ TB/hour}
$$

> **Insight:** This single data center generates **~25 TB/day**, exceeding traditional RDBMS capacity and validating the need for Big Data infrastructure (HDFS, S3, HBase).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Big Data. List any four of its characteristics.**
*(Expected length: 3–4 lines, 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Big Data** refers to extremely large, fast-moving, and diverse datasets that cannot be processed by traditional database systems using conventional tools. It is characterized by the **5 V's**: **Volume** (scale), **Velocity** (speed of generation), **Variety** (different data types), **Veracity** (data quality), and **Value** (business insight extracted).
>
> *(Any 4 V's earn 3 marks; all 5 V's earn full marks with extra credit.)*

**Q2. Differentiate between structured and unstructured data with one Industry 4.0 example each.**
*(Expected length: 4–5 lines, 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Structured Data | Unstructured Data |
|---|---|---|
| Format | Tabular, fixed schema (rows/columns) | No fixed schema (free-form) |
| Storage | RDBMS (SQL) | Data lakes, NoSQL |
| Example | CNC machine temperature logs in SQL | Surveillance video footage from smart factory |
| % of Big Data | ~20% | ~80% |

> Structured data is organized and easily queryable, while unstructured data lacks a predefined model and requires advanced techniques (NLP, computer vision) for analysis. *(3 marks for a clear contrast + 1 example each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE University Exam — Module 3 Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q (a)** Explain in detail the **5 V's of Big Data** with suitable Industry 4.0 examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The 5 V's form the foundational framework for understanding Big Data characteristics:

**1. Volume (Scale):** Refers to the magnitude of data generated. In Industry 4.0, a single smart factory may deploy **10,000+ IoT sensors** producing terabytes of telemetry daily. Globally, IDC estimates data will reach **181 ZB by 2025**.

**2. Velocity (Speed):** Refers to the rate at which data is produced and must be processed. Autonomous guided vehicles (AGVs) in a smart warehouse require sensor fusion at **< 50 ms** latency for obstacle avoidance.

**3. Variety (Format Diversity):** Refers to heterogeneous data types. A predictive maintenance system ingests vibration waveforms (structured), maintenance manuals (text), and infrared thermal images (unstructured).

**4. Veracity (Quality):** Refers to data trustworthiness. Sensor calibration drift, electrical interference, and missing values reduce data quality. Veracity-aware pipelines apply **Kalman filtering** and **anomaly detection** to improve reliability.

**5. Value (Business Utility):** Refers to actionable insights derived from analytics. The end goal is to convert raw data into business value — for example, reducing unplanned downtime by 30–50% through predictive maintenance.

> *Valuation: 1.4 marks per V (definition + example). Add a concluding sentence for full 7 marks.*

**Q (b)** Compare **traditional RDBMS** with **Big Data systems**. Why is RDBMS inadequate for Industry 4.0 applications? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Traditional RDBMS | Big Data Systems |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Centralized, vertical scaling | Distributed, horizontal scaling |
| Data Volume | GB to few TB | **PB to ZB** |
| Data Type | Structured only | Structured + Semi + Unstructured |
| Schema | Schema-on-write (rigid) | Schema-on-read (flexible) |
| Transaction | ACID compliant | **BASE (eventual consistency)** |
| Processing | Batch, slow | Batch + Real-time stream |
| Hardware | High-end proprietary servers | Commodity hardware clusters |
| Fault Tolerance | RAID-based, limited | Native replication, self-healing |
| Cost per TB | High | Low (open-source: Hadoop, Spark) |
| Examples | Oracle, MySQL, SQL Server | HDFS, HBase, Cassandra, Spark |

**Why RDBMS is Inadequate for Industry 4.0:**

1. **Volume limit:** A single-node RDBMS cannot scale to PB/ZB.
2. **Velocity limit:** RDBMS is not designed for sub-second streaming.
3. **Variety limit:** It cannot handle unstructured images, video, or sensor streams natively.
4. **Schema rigidity:** Industry 4.0 data is "schema-on-read" — schema evolves dynamically.
5. **Cost:** High-end proprietary licensing becomes prohibitive at scale.

> *Valuation: 3.5 marks for table + 3.5 marks for the 5-point argument. Conclude with a one-line statement: "Hence, Big Data platforms like Hadoop/Spark are essential for Industry 4.0."*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**Q (a)** Discuss the **role of Big Data in Industry 4.0**. Explain with at least three real-world application scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Big Data in Industry 4.0:**
Big Data acts as the *central nervous system* of Industry 4.0. It enables real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, autonomous decision-making, and continuous optimization of cyber-physical systems.

**Three Real-World Application Scenarios:**

1. **Predictive Maintenance in Smart Manufacturing:**
   - IoT sensors on CNC machines stream vibration, temperature, and acoustic data.
   - Big Data platforms (Kafka → Spark → MLlib) ingest and analyze these streams.
   - ML models predict equipment failure 24–72 hours in advance.
   - **Outcome:** 30–50% reduction in unplanned downtime, 10–25% reduction in maintenance cost (Deloitte benchmark).

2. **Supply Chain Optimization:**
   - Real-time data from GPS, RFID, weather APIs, and demand logs are aggregated.
   - Big Data analytics optimize routing, inventory levels, and demand forecasting.
   - **Outcome:** 15–20% reduction in logistics costs; just-in-time delivery accuracy > 95%.

3. **Smart Grid and Energy Management:**
   - Smart meters generate high-velocity consumption data every 15 minutes.
   - Big Data platforms perform load forecasting, peak shaving, and demand-response analytics.
   - **Outcome:** 10–15% energy savings; improved grid stability and renewable integration.

> *Valuation: 1 mark for the role + 2 marks per scenario (description + outcome) = 7 marks.*

**Q (b)** A smart city deploys **10,000 IoT air-quality sensors**, each sending **1 KB of data every 5 seconds**, to a centralized Big Data platform. Calculate:
   - (i) The total data generated per day.
   - (ii) Whether this constitutes "Big Data" by Volume criterion, with justification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Number of records per sensor per day**

$$
R_{sensor} = \frac{86400 \text{ s}}{5 \text{ s}} = 17280 \text{ records/day}
$$

**Step 2: Total records across all sensors**

$$
R_{total} = 10000 \times 17280 = 1.728 \times 10^{8} \text{ records/day}
$$

**Step 3: Total data volume per day**

$$
V_{day} = 1.728 \times 10^{8} \times 1 \text{ KB} = 1.728 \times 10^{8} \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to GB**

$$
V_{day} = \frac{1.728 \times 10^{8}}{10^{6}} = 172.8 \text{ GB/day} \approx 0.173 \text{ TB/day}
$$

**Step 5: Annual projection**

$$
V_{year} = 0.173 \times 365 \approx 63.1 \text{ TB/year}
$$

**(ii) Justification — Is this "Big Data" by Volume?**
- **Yes, in aggregate context.** 63 TB/year is approaching the Petabyte scale when combined with historical retention (5–10 years → 0.3–0.6 PB) and additional data sources (traffic, weather, video).
- More importantly, when combined with **Velocity** (5-second interval ≈ real-time) and **Variety** (sensor + video + weather data), it clearly satisfies the multi-V definition of Big Data.
- Even at 172 GB/day, traditional RDBMS struggles with concurrent ingestion, real-time analytics, and historical queries across years of data.

> *Valuation: 4 marks for the calculation steps + 3 marks for the justification (Volume + Velocity + Variety synthesis).*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Big Data Questions:**
> 1. **Naming only 3 V's instead of 5** — Always list Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, **and** Value. The 5th V is frequently asked as a discriminator question.
> 2. **Writing "Big Data = Lots of Data"** — This is *not* a definition. You lose 1–2 marks. Use Gartner's formal definition.
> 3. **Confusing "Big Data" with "Data Science"** — Big Data is the *infrastructure/storage/processing layer*; Data Science is the *analytics/ML layer*. Examiners deduct marks for conflating the two.
> 4. **Skipping the numerical calculation steps** — Always show unit conversions (KB → MB → GB → TB) explicitly. Skipping intermediate steps costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to cite Industry 4.0 context** — The course is OEAET832 (Industry 4.0); pure generic answers without smart-factory/IoT examples lose contextual marks.
> 6. **Spelling "Hadoop" as "Hadop" or "HDFS" as "HFDS"** — Technical term spelling is a silent mark-killer in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Big Data = high-volume + high-velocity + high-variety data assets (Gartner).
- ✅ **5 V's:** **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value** — always write all five for full marks.
- ✅ **Volume units:** KB → MB → GB → TB → PB → EB → ZB → YB (each step = $10^{3}$).
- ✅ **Variety types:** Structured (~20%) / Semi-structured (JSON, XML) / Unstructured (~80%).
- ✅ **Why RDBMS fails:** Single-node, schema-rigid, ACID-only, no real-time, no unstructured handling.
- ✅ **Big Data architecture:** Distributed, horizontally scalable, BASE, schema-on-read.
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 + Big Data:** Predictive maintenance, smart grids, digital twins, supply chain.
- ✅ **Key technologies:** Hadoop (HDFS + MapReduce), Spark, Kafka, Flink, HBase, Cassandra.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem:** $C + A + P \leq 2$ — Big Data systems typically choose AP (Availability + Partition tolerance).
- ✅ **Storage formula:** $C_{safe} = \dfrac{N_{nodes} \times C_{node} \times (1 - f_{reserved})}{RF}$
- ✅ **Throughput formula:** $R = \dfrac{D}{t}$ (data rate).
- ✅ **Real-time latency bound:** $\leq 100$ ms for Industry 4.0 closed-loop control.
- ✅ **MapReduce Amdahl bound:** $S_{p} \leq N_{workers}$ — speedup never exceeds parallelism.
- ✅ **HDFS default block size:** 64 MB or 128 MB; default replication factor = 3.
- ✅ **Zettabyte projection:** ~181 ZB globally by 2025 (IDC/Statista).
- ✅ **Always contextualize** answers within Industry 4.0 / smart factory / IoT framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For any 14-mark question in this module, structure your answer as **(i) Definition/Concept → (ii) 5 V's with examples → (iii) RDBMS vs. Big Data comparison → (iv) Industry 4.0 application → (v) Conclusion with one numerical example.** This template guarantees a high score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Big Data Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET832 - INDUSTRY 4.0
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Big Data Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Big Data Sources** refer to the diverse origins, channels, and systems from which massive volumes of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data are continuously generated, captured, and made available for analytical processing in an Industry 4.0 ecosystem. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEAET832 - Industry 4.0, Module 3), Big Data sources constitute the foundational input layer of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), enabling real-time decision-making, predictive analytics, and intelligent automation across manufacturing, healthcare, retail, transportation, and smart city applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the context of Industry 4.0, Big Data Sources are classified by their **origin domain** (human-generated vs. machine-generated), **data structure** (structured, semi-structured, unstructured), **generation velocity** (batch vs. streaming), and **value chain position** (operational, transactional, behavioral).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a massive **municipal water treatment plant**. The plant doesn't manufacture water; instead, it receives water from hundreds of different sources — rivers, lakes, wells, rainwater tanks, industrial effluents, and household drainage pipes. Each source has a different quality, flow rate, chemical composition, and contamination level. Before the water can be useful (drinking, irrigation, industrial use), the plant must **filter, classify, and process** these diverse inflows.

**Big Data works identically:**
- The **water plant** = Big Data Analytics Platform (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL databases)
- The **rivers, lakes, wells, drainage pipes** = Big Data Sources (Social Media, IoT Sensors, Transactions, Logs, etc.)
- The **water quality parameters** (pH, turbidity, chemical content) = The 5 V's of Big Data (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value)
- The **treatment process** = Big Data Processing (Cleaning, Transformation, Analysis)

Just as the plant needs to know **where the water is coming from** to apply the right treatment, an Industry 4.0 engineer must **classify Big Data sources** to apply the right analytics, storage, and governance mechanisms.

### 1.3 The 5 V's of Big Data — KTU Standard Metrics

The characterization of any Big Data source is governed by five universal dimensions, formally codified by IBM and adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Dimension | Definition | Engineering Metric |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| **Volume** | Scale of data generated | Terabytes to Zettabytes ($10^{21}$ bytes) |
| **Velocity** | Speed of data generation and processing | Batch, Near-Real-Time, Real-Time (ms latency) |
| **Variety** | Different data formats and types | Structured, Semi-Structured, Unstructured |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness and quality of data | Accuracy, Provenance, Completeness score |
| **Value** | Business/engineering insight extracted | ROI, Predictive accuracy, Process optimization % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** Global data creation is projected to exceed **180 Zettabytes (ZB)** by 2025, with industrial IoT sensors contributing approximately **30%** of this total volume — a critical figure for KTU numerical problems.

### 1.4 Classification Framework — First Principles

Big Data Sources are broadly classified into **three primary domains**:

1. **Human-Generated Sources** — Data produced by human interactions (social media posts, emails, multimedia uploads, mobile applications).
2. **Machine-Generated Sources** — Data produced autonomously by sensors, devices, and machines (IoT sensors, server logs, RFID readings, telemetry).
3. **Process-Generated / Organizational Sources** — Data produced by business and operational processes (transactional databases, ERP systems, supply chain records).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deconstruction of Big Data Sources — Structured Logic

Big Data Sources are deconstructed along **four orthogonal axes** that determine their engineering treatment:

**Axis 1: Source Origin (Who/What generates the data?)**

- **Human-Driven Sources:** Social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter/X, Instagram, LinkedIn, YouTube), mobile applications, customer feedback systems, call center recordings, e-commerce reviews.
- **Machine-Driven Sources:** IoT sensors (temperature, pressure, vibration, humidity), SCADA systems, RFID tags, GPS trackers, smart meters, autonomous vehicle telemetry, industrial PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers).
- **Process-Driven Sources:** Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems, Customer Relationship Management (CRM) platforms, Supply Chain Management (SCM) systems, banking transaction ledgers, healthcare Electronic Medical Records (EMR).

**Axis 2: Data Structure (What format is the data in?)**

- **Structured Data:** Highly organized, schema-defined, tabular format. Example: SQL databases, CSV files, sensor readings stored in time-series tables. Occupies approximately **20%** of enterprise data.
- **Semi-Structured Data:** Partial organization with flexible schema. Example: JSON, XML, log files, NoSQL documents, email metadata. Occupies approximately **30%** of enterprise data.
- **Unstructured Data:** No predefined schema. Example: Images, videos, audio files, free-text documents, social media posts. Occupies approximately **80%** of all global data generated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** The "80% unstructured" figure is a high-yield statistic — examiners frequently test whether students can correctly associate data types (image, video, sensor reading) with their structural classification.

**Axis 3: Generation Velocity (How fast is data produced?)**

- **Batch Sources:** Data accumulated and processed periodically (daily, weekly). Example: Daily sales reports, monthly billing systems.
- **Near-Real-Time Sources:** Data processed within seconds to minutes. Example: Clickstream analysis, recommendation engines.
- **Real-Time / Streaming Sources:** Data processed in milliseconds. Example: High-frequency trading, industrial process control, autonomous driving, anomaly detection in manufacturing.

**Axis 4: Storage and Processing Compatibility**

- **Hot Data Sources:** Require immediate access and processing. Stored in in-memory databases (Redis, Apache Ignite).
- **Warm Data Sources:** Accessed occasionally. Stored in NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Cassandra).
- **Cold Data Sources:** Rarely accessed, archival. Stored in data lakes (HDFS, Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Representation | Application Context |
|-------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Data Volume Order | $10^{n}$ bytes, $n \in \{3,6,9,12,15,18,21\}$ | KB, MB, GB, TB, PB, EB, ZB |
| Streaming Throughput | $T = \frac{D}{t}$ where $D$ = data size, $t$ = time | Real-time sensor pipeline capacity |
| Storage Requirement | $S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \times R_r$ where $R_r$ = replication factor (typically **3** for HDFS) | Hadoop cluster sizing |
| Data Variety Index | $V_{idx} = \frac{N_{unstr}}{N_{total}}$ where $N_{unstr}$ = unstructured records, $N_{total}$ = total records | Measuring heterogeneity of source mix |
| Veracity Score | $V_{score} = 1 - \frac{E_{count}}{T_{count}}$ where $E_{count}$ = erroneous records, $T_{count}$ = total records | Data quality assessment |
| Source Diversity Coefficient | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2(p_i)$ (Shannon Entropy) | Quantifying diversity of data sources |
| IoT Data Rate | $R_{iot} = N_{sensors} \times f_s \times b_s$ where $N$ = sensors, $f_s$ = sampling frequency (Hz), $b_s$ = bits per sample | Industrial sensor network sizing |
| Data Growth Rate | $G(t) = G_0 \times e^{rt}$ (Exponential model) | Long-term capacity planning |

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Concept

**Why classify Big Data Sources?**

In an Industry 4.0 smart factory, an engineer cannot apply the same analytical technique to a vibration sensor reading as to a customer tweet. The classification framework ensures **right tool for the right data** — structured SQL data goes to relational warehouses, while unstructured image data requires deep learning pipelines.

**How do these formulas enable engineering decisions?**

- The **Storage Requirement** formula $S_{total} = \sum D_i \times R_r$ directly determines the number of physical hard disks required in a Hadoop DataNode cluster.
- The **Source Diversity Coefficient** $H$ (Shannon Entropy) quantifies how diversified an organization's data ingestion portfolio is — critical for risk management in data strategy.
- The **IoT Data Rate** formula $R_{iot}$ enables accurate bandwidth provisioning for industrial wireless sensor networks (WSN).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Bosch, Siemens, and General Electric use these exact classification principles in their **Predix, MindSphere, and Brilliant Factory** platforms respectively, to route data from thousands of sensors to the appropriate analytics engines.

### 2.4 Detailed Inventory of Big Data Sources (KTU Board-Favorite Content)

**A. Social Media Platforms (Human-Generated, Unstructured)**

- **Twitter/X:** 500+ million tweets/day, 280-character limit, JSON format, high velocity.
- **Facebook/Meta:** 4+ Petabytes of data generated daily — photos, videos, comments, likes, shares.
- **YouTube:** 500+ hours of video uploaded every minute — predominantly unstructured multimedia.
- **Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, Snapchat:** Image-heavy, location-tagged, engagement-metric rich.

**B. Internet of Things (IoT) Sensors (Machine-Generated, Time-Series Structured)**

- **Industrial Sensors:** Temperature, pressure, flow rate, vibration, humidity, voltage, current.
- **Smart Wearables:** Heart rate, GPS, accelerometer, gyroscope (Fitbit, Apple Watch, medical devices).
- **Smart City Sensors:** Traffic flow, air quality (PM2.5, CO2, NOx), noise levels, waste bin fill levels.
- **Connected Vehicles:** CAN bus data, OBD-II diagnostics, GPS trails, LIDAR point clouds.

**C. Transactional Systems (Process-Generated, Structured)**

- **Point-of-Sale (POS) Systems:** Retail transaction logs, barcode scans, payment records.
- **Banking & Financial Systems:** SWIFT transfers, credit card swipes, ATM logs, stock exchange ticks.
- **E-Commerce Platforms:** Order records, cart abandonment logs, product reviews, clickstream.
- **ERP/CRM Systems:** SAP, Oracle, Salesforce — master data, transaction data, audit logs.

**D. Machine & Server Logs (Machine-Generated, Semi-Structured)**

- **Application Logs:** Web server access logs (Apache, Nginx), application error traces.
- **System Logs:** OS event logs, kernel logs, syslog, Windows Event Viewer.
- **Network Logs:** Firewall logs, DNS queries, intrusion detection system (IDS) alerts.
- **CDN & Cloud Logs:** AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Logs, Google Cloud Operations.

**E. Multimedia Content (Human-Generated, Unstructured)**

- **Images:** Surveillance cameras (CCTV), satellite imagery, medical X-rays/MRIs/CT scans.
- **Videos:** CCTV footage, drone footage, video conferencing recordings.
- **Audio:** Voice calls, call center recordings, voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant).

**F. Public and Open Data Sources (Government/Research, Semi-Structured)**

- **Government Portals:** data.gov, data.gov.in, European Data Portal.
- **Scientific Datasets:** CERN LHC data, NASA Earth Observing System, genomic databases (NCBI, Ensembl).
- **Weather Data:** NOAA, IMD (India Meteorological Department), OpenWeatherMap API.

**G. Mobile and Location-Based Data (Human-Generated, Semi-Structured)**

- **GPS Traces:** Google Maps, Uber/Ola ride data, fitness apps.
- **Mobile App Telemetry:** Crash reports, in-app behavior, push notification responses.
- **Telecom CDR (Call Detail Records):** Call duration, SMS logs, cell tower handovers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1: Total Data Volume from IoT Sensor Network

**Problem Statement:** A smart factory deploys $N_{sensors} = 500$ IoT vibration sensors. Each sensor samples at $f_s = 1000$ Hz (samples per second), with each sample encoded at $b_s = 16$ bits. The factory operates for $t = 8$ hours/day, $d = 300$ days/year. Calculate the annual raw data volume in TB.

**Step 1: Establish the core formula**

The data rate per sensor is given by:

$$
R_{sensor} = f_s \times b_s
$$

where:
- $f_s$ = sampling frequency in samples/second (Hz)
- $b_s$ = bits per sample

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values**

$$
R_{sensor} = 1000 \times 16 = 16{,}000 \text{ bits/second}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to bytes per second**

$$
R_{sensor\_bytes} = \frac{16{,}000}{8} = 2{,}000 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate data rate for the entire sensor network**

$$
R_{network} = N_{sensors} \times R_{sensor\_bytes}
$$

$$
R_{network} = 500 \times 2{,}000 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes/second} = 1 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate daily data volume**

$$
D_{daily} = R_{network} \times t \times 3600
$$

$$
D_{daily} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 8 \times 3600 = 2.88 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes/day} = 28.8 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate annual data volume**

$$
D_{annual} = D_{daily} \times d
$$

$$
D_{annual} = 28.8 \times 300 = 8{,}640 \text{ GB} = 8.64 \text{ TB/year}
$$

**Step 7: Account for replication factor $R_r = 3$ (HDFS default)**

$$
S_{total} = D_{annual} \times R_r
$$

$$
S_{total} = 8.64 \times 3 = 25.92 \text{ TB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This single calculation justifies why Hadoop clusters in Industry 4.0 are provisioned with replication factor 3 — it triples storage but ensures fault tolerance, a non-negotiable requirement in continuous manufacturing.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2: Source Diversity using Shannon Entropy

**Problem Statement:** An e-commerce company ingests data from 4 sources with the following daily data proportions: Social Media (50%), Transaction Logs (25%), Clickstream (15%), Customer Reviews (10%). Calculate the Source Diversity Coefficient using Shannon Entropy.

**Step 1: Define the Shannon Entropy formula**

$$
H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2(p_i)
$$

where $p_i$ is the probability (proportion) of each source.

**Step 2: Identify the probabilities**

$$
p_1 = 0.50, \quad p_2 = 0.25, \quad p_3 = 0.15, \quad p_4 = 0.10
$$

**Step 3: Compute each term**

$$
H_1 = -0.50 \times \log_2(0.50)
$$

$$
\log_2(0.50) = -1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_1 = -0.50 \times (-1.0) = 0.500
$$

$$
H_2 = -0.25 \times \log_2(0.25)
$$

$$
\log_2(0.25) = -2.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_2 = -0.25 \times (-2.0) = 0.500
$$

$$
H_3 = -0.15 \times \log_2(0.15)
$$

$$
\log_2(0.15) = -2.737 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_3 = -0.15 \times (-2.737) = 0.411
$$

$$
H_4 = -0.10 \times \log_2(0.10)
$$

$$
\log_2(0.10) = -3.322 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_4 = -0.10 \times (-3.322) = 0.332
$$

**Step 4: Sum all contributions**

$$
H = 0.500 + 0.500 + 0.411 + 0.332
$$

$$
H = 1.743 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result**

The maximum possible entropy for 4 sources (uniform distribution) is:

$$
H_{max} = \log_2(4) = 2.0 \text{ bits}
$$

Therefore, the normalized diversity is:

$$
H_{norm} = \frac{H}{H_{max}} = \frac{1.743}{2.0} = 0.8715 \approx 87.15\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A normalized entropy of 87.15% indicates a **highly diversified** data source portfolio — the company is not over-reliant on any single source, which reduces strategic risk and enhances analytical robustness.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3: Veracity Score for Streaming Sensor Data

**Problem Statement:** A predictive maintenance system receives 10,000 sensor readings per hour. Statistical analysis reveals 127 readings are erroneous (outlier values due to sensor drift), 43 are missing, and 58 are duplicates. Calculate the Veracity Score.

**Step 1: Define the Veracity Score formula**

$$
V_{score} = 1 - \frac{E_{count}}{T_{count}}
$$

where:
- $E_{count}$ = total erroneous (anomalous) records
- $T_{count}$ = total records received

**Step 2: Sum all data quality issues**

$$
E_{count} = E_{erroneous} + E_{missing} + E_{duplicate}
$$

$$
E_{count} = 127 + 43 + 58 = 228
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the formula**

$$
V_{score} = 1 - \frac{228}{10{,}000}
$$

$$
V_{score} = 1 - 0.0228 = 0.9772
$$

**Step 4: Express as percentage**

$$
V_{score} = 97.72\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Context:** In Industry 4.0 predictive maintenance, a Veracity Score below **95%** is typically flagged as unacceptable, triggering automated sensor recalibration alerts in the SCADA system.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4: Data Growth Projection

**Problem Statement:** A smart city's data lake currently holds $G_0 = 50$ TB. The annual growth rate is $r = 0.35$ (35% year-over-year). Project the data volume after 5 years using the exponential growth model.

**Step 1: State the exponential growth formula**

$$
G(t) = G_0 \times e^{rt}
$$

where:
- $G_0$ = initial data volume
- $r$ = annual growth rate (decimal)
- $t$ = time in years
- $e$ = Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$

**Step 2: Substitute values for $t = 5$**

$$
G(5) = 50 \times e^{0.35 \times 5}
$$

$$
G(5) = 50 \times e^{1.75}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the exponential term**

$$
e^{1.75} = 5.7546
$$

**Step 4: Compute final volume**

$$
G(5) = 50 \times 5.7546 = 287.73 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the growth multiplier**

$$
\text{Growth Multiplier} = \frac{G(5)}{G_0} = \frac{287.73}{50} \approx 5.75
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This projection justifies capital expenditure for scalable cloud storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob) versus fixed on-premise infrastructure, a critical boardroom decision in Industry 4.0 digital transformation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Source-to-Technology Mapping

| Big Data Source | Data Type | Velocity | Volume/day | Recommended Storage | Recommended Processing |
|------------------|-----------|----------|------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| Social Media (Twitter) | Unstructured JSON | Real-Time | ~500M tweets | MongoDB / Cassandra | Apache Spark Streaming |
| IoT Vibration Sensors | Structured Time-Series | Real-Time | ~10 GB | InfluxDB / TimescaleDB | Apache Flink / Kafka Streams |
| E-Commerce Transactions | Structured SQL | Batch + Real-Time | ~50 GB | MySQL / PostgreSQL | Apache Hive / Spark SQL |
| CCTV Surveillance Video | Unstructured Video | Streaming | ~5 TB | AWS S3 / Hadoop HDFS | OpenCV + Deep Learning |
| Web Server Logs | Semi-Structured Text | Near-Real-Time | ~2 GB | Elasticsearch | ELK Stack (Logstash + Kibana) |
| ERP/CRM Records | Structured SQL | Batch | ~1 GB | Oracle / SAP HANA | SAP BW / Power BI |
| Medical Imaging (MRI/CT) | Unstructured DICOM | Batch | ~100 GB | PACS / Cloud Archive | Deep Learning (CNN) |
| GPS/Telematics | Semi-Structured | Real-Time | ~500 MB | Cassandra / Redis | Apache Storm / Spark |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Big Data Sources. List any four major categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Big Data Sources are the diverse origins from which massive volumes of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data are generated, captured, and ingested into analytics platforms for Industry 4.0 applications.

**Four Major Categories:**

1. **Human-Generated Sources:** Social media platforms (e.g., Twitter — 500 million tweets/day).
2. **Machine-Generated Sources:** IoT sensors in smart factories (e.g., vibration sensors at 1000 Hz sampling).
3. **Process-Generated Sources:** Enterprise systems (e.g., SAP ERP transaction logs).
4. **Multimedia Sources:** Video surveillance systems (e.g., CCTV footage at 5 TB/day).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Explain the 5 V's of Big Data in the context of Industry 4.0 with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

The 5 V's characterize every Big Data Source:

1. **Volume:** Scale of data. *Example:* Global data generation exceeds 180 Zettabytes by 2025.
2. **Velocity:** Speed of generation. *Example:* Real-time vibration sensors in predictive maintenance sampling at 1000 Hz.
3. **Variety:** Different formats. *Example:* SQL tables (structured), JSON logs (semi-structured), CCTV video (unstructured).
4. **Veracity:** Data quality and trustworthiness. *Example:* SCADA sensor data has higher veracity than crowdsourced social media data.
5. **Value:** Business insight extracted. *Example:* Predictive maintenance reduces equipment downtime by 30%, saving millions annually.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Classify Big Data Sources in detail along four axes: origin, structure, velocity, and value chain position. Give at least two examples for each classification axis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A smart manufacturing plant deploys 800 IoT temperature sensors, each sampling at 500 Hz with 12-bit resolution. The plant operates 16 hours/day for 350 days/year. Calculate: (i) data rate per sensor in KB/s, (ii) total daily data volume in GB, (iii) annual storage requirement with HDFS replication factor of 3. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Axis 1: Origin (Generator)**

- **Human-Generated:** Twitter feeds (500M tweets/day), Instagram uploads (100M photos/day).
- **Machine-Generated:** Temperature sensors (this case), RFID tags on assembly line.
- **Process-Generated:** SAP ERP master data, banking SWIFT transactions.

**Axis 2: Structure**

- **Structured:** SQL tables, sensor readings in time-series format.
- **Semi-Structured:** JSON API responses, Apache log files, XML invoices.
- **Unstructured:** CCTV footage, voice recordings, free-text customer reviews.

**Axis 3: Velocity**

- **Batch:** Monthly financial reports, daily sales aggregates.
- **Near-Real-Time:** Recommendation engines, clickstream analysis.
- **Real-Time:** Vibration sensors, autonomous vehicle LIDAR, high-frequency trading.

**Axis 4: Value Chain Position**

- **Operational:** Real-time machine control, process optimization.
- **Managerial:** KPIs, dashboards, monthly performance reviews.
- **Strategic:** Long-term trend analysis, market forecasting, R&D.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Data rate per sensor:**

$$
R_{sensor} = f_s \times b_s = 500 \times 12 = 6{,}000 \text{ bits/second}
$$

$$
R_{sensor\_bytes} = \frac{6{,}000}{8} = 750 \text{ bytes/s} = 0.732 \text{ KB/s}
$$

**(ii) Total daily data volume:**

$$
R_{network} = 800 \times 0.732 = 585.94 \text{ KB/s}
$$

$$
D_{daily} = 585.94 \times 16 \times 3600 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
D_{daily} = 585.94 \times 57{,}600 = 33{,}750{,}000 \text{ KB} \approx 32.19 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**(iii) Annual storage with replication:**

$$
D_{annual} = 32.19 \times 350 = 11{,}266.5 \text{ GB} \approx 11.0 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
S_{total} = 11.0 \times 3 = 33.0 \text{ TB}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with a real-world analogy the concept of Big Data Sources and the 5 V's. Discuss how an Industry 4.0 engineer would classify the data sources in a smart factory. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce platform receives data from 5 sources with proportions: Product Catalog (40%), Customer Reviews (25%), Clickstream (20%), Payment Logs (10%), Support Chat (5%). Calculate the Shannon Entropy and normalized diversity. Comment on the strategic implication. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Real-World Analogy — Water Treatment Plant:**

A municipal water treatment plant receives water from rivers (continuous source), lakes (batch source), industrial effluents (semi-structured source), and rainwater (unpredictable source). Each requires different filtration. Similarly, Big Data Sources (sensors, social media, transactions, logs) require different storage and analytics — this is the essence of Big Data Source classification.

**Smart Factory Data Source Classification:**

1. **Machine Sensors (Temperature, Pressure, Vibration):** Structured, real-time, high veracity, high operational value.
2. **Production Line Cameras:** Unstructured video, real-time streaming, used for visual quality inspection.
3. **ERP System (SAP):** Structured transactional, batch-processed, used for managerial decisions.
4. **Supply Chain RFID Tags:** Semi-structured, near-real-time, used for inventory tracking.
5. **Operator Wearables:** Semi-structured biometric + GPS, real-time, used for safety monitoring.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $p_1 = 0.40, p_2 = 0.25, p_3 = 0.20, p_4 = 0.10, p_5 = 0.05$

**Compute each term:**

$$
H_1 = -0.40 \times \log_2(0.40) = -0.40 \times (-1.322) = 0.529
$$

$$
H_2 = -0.25 \times \log_2(0.25) = -0.25 \times (-2.000) = 0.500
$$

$$
H_3 = -0.20 \times \log_2(0.20) = -0.20 \times (-2.322) = 0.464
$$

$$
H_4 = -0.10 \times \log_2(0.10) = -0.10 \times (-3.322) = 0.332
$$

$$
H_5 = -0.05 \times \log_2(0.05) = -0.05 \times (-4.322) = 0.216
$$

**Total Entropy:**

$$
H = 0.529 + 0.500 + 0.464 + 0.332 + 0.216 = 2.041 \text{ bits}
$$

**Maximum Entropy:**

$$
H_{max} = \log_2(5) = 2.322 \text{ bits}
$$

**Normalized Diversity:**

$$
H_{norm} = \frac{2.041}{2.322} = 0.879 \approx 87.9\%
$$

**Strategic Implication:** A normalized diversity of 87.9% indicates a **well-balanced** data source portfolio. The platform is not over-dependent on any single source, providing resilience against data loss from any one channel. However, the dominant Product Catalog source (40%) suggests the company should invest in robust catalog management systems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** Students frequently forget to convert bits to bytes (divide by 8), or seconds to hours (multiply by 3600), or KB to GB (divide by $10^6$). Always show unit conversions explicitly.
>
> 2. **Replication Factor Oversight:** In HDFS storage calculations, students often compute raw data volume but forget the replication factor of 3. Examiners allocate 1-2 marks specifically for this.
>
> 3. **Confusing Structured vs. Unstructured:** Email is **semi-structured** (header + body), not unstructured. A photograph is unstructured. A SQL row is structured. Students lose marks for misclassification.
>
> 4. **Shannon Entropy Sign Error:** Students forget the negative sign in $H = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)$, producing nonsensical negative entropy values.
>
> 5. **Missing Real-Time Examples:** When asked for examples, students often give generic answers. KTU examiners reward **Industry 4.0-specific** examples (smart factory, IIoT, digital twin) over generic ones.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Veracity and Value:** Many students only mention 3 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety). The full 5 V's including **Veracity** and **Value** are mandatory in KTU 2024 Scheme.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Big Data Sources**
>
> - Big Data Sources are the **origin layer** of the Industry 4.0 data value chain.
> - **Three primary source categories:** Human-Generated, Machine-Generated, Process-Generated.
> - **5 V's mandatory:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
> - **80% of global data is unstructured** — high-yield statistic for KTU exams.
> - **IoT sensors are the dominant source** in Industry 4.0 (contribute ~30% of global data).
> - **Standard formula:** $R = f_s \times b_s$ for sensor data rate; $S_{total} = D \times R_r$ for HDFS storage with replication.
> - **Shannon Entropy** $H = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)$ quantifies source diversity.
> - **Veracity Score** $V = 1 - E/T$ should exceed 95% for industrial applications.
> - **Real-time sources** require stream processing (Kafka, Flink); **batch sources** use Hadoop MapReduce/Spark.
> - **Data Lake** stores raw data (HDFS, S3); **Data Warehouse** stores processed structured data (Snowflake, Redshift).
> - **Structured data** → SQL; **Semi-structured** → NoSQL (MongoDB); **Unstructured** → Deep Learning pipelines.
> - **Smart factory sensors** are the canonical Industry 4.0 Big Data Source example.
> - **Common Big Data Sources in KTU context:** Twitter/Facebook (social), RFID/sensors (IoT), SAP/Oracle (ERP), Apache logs (web), CCTV (multimedia).
> - **Velocity tiers:** Batch (hours-days), Near-Real-Time (seconds-minutes), Real-Time (milliseconds).
> - **Storage tiers:** Hot (Redis), Warm (NoSQL), Cold (Data Lake/Archive).
> - Always show **unit conversion steps** in numerical problems to secure full marks.
> - Always mention **replication factor** in HDFS storage calculations.
> - In classification questions, use **four axes:** Origin, Structure, Velocity, Value Chain Position.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Big Data Acquisition and Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET832 - INDUSTRY 4.0
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Big Data Acquisition and Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Big Data Acquisition and Storage?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Big Data Acquisition** is the systematic process of **identifying, capturing, ingesting, transporting, and staging** massive volumes of heterogeneous data from disparate distributed sources (sensors, machines, social feeds, transactional logs, IoT devices, enterprise systems) into a centralized or distributed processing/storage infrastructure. **Big Data Storage** refers to the **distributed, scalable, fault-tolerant persistence architectures** (HDFS, NoSQL, Data Lakes, Cloud Object Stores) engineered to hold, retrieve, and manage Petabyte-to-Exabyte scale datasets while satisfying the **5 V** characteristics of Big Data.

In the **Industry 4.0** context, every sensor on a smart factory floor, every PLC register, every vibration reading from a CNC spindle, every RFID scan, every ERP transaction — is a **data source**. Acquisition is the act of *listening* to all of them simultaneously. Storage is the act of *remembering* all of them indefinitely in a way that future AI/ML models can query efficiently.

### 1.2 The 5 V's of Big Data — The Foundational Pillars

Every KTU question on this topic revolves around the **5 V's**. Memorize the table below cold.

| V Characteristic | Plain-English Meaning | Industry 4.0 Example | Unit / Scale Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Sheer *size* of data | 1 million vibration samples/sec from a turbine | **Petabytes (PB)**, **Exabytes (EB)**, **Zettabytes (ZB)** |
| **Velocity** | *Speed* at which data arrives | 100,000 RFID reads/sec on a conveyor | **Mbps**, **Gbps**, **events/sec** |
| **Variety** | *Different formats* (structured, semi, unstructured) | Sensor CSV + CCTV video + maintenance JSON logs | Number of **schemas / formats** |
| **Veracity** | *Trustworthiness / noise* in data | A faulty thermocouple reporting 9000 °C | **% data quality**, **confidence score** |
| **Value** | *Business insight* extractable | Predictive maintenance saves ₹50 lakh/year | **ROI %**, **insight density** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern Alert:** The 5 V's are asked **every single year**. Always mention *all five* and give **one Industry 4.0 example for each**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital" Intuition

Imagine a **Smart Hospital** with 10,000 patients, each wearing 5 IoT sensors (heart rate, BP, SpO2, temperature, accelerometer).

- **Acquisition** = The nurses' station receiving a continuous flood of signals — some arriving every millisecond (ECG), some once a day (lab reports), some as images (X-rays). The station must *catch all of them without dropping any*.
- **Storage** = The hospital's massive medical archive — but instead of one giant filing room, it's split across **50 distributed filing cabinets** (servers), each with **3 copies** of every file so that if one cabinet burns down, the data survives. A smart indexing system lets doctors pull up any patient's 10-year history in 2 seconds.

That's Big Data Acquisition and Storage in a nutshell: **catch everything, store it everywhere safely, and retrieve it instantly.**

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Industry 4.0

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Without robust acquisition and storage, **no** downstream Industry 4.0 technology works — no Digital Twin (needs historical sensor logs), no Predictive Maintenance (needs failure-mode datasets), no AI-driven quality control (needs labelled image archives), no supply-chain optimization (needs transactional + GPS streams). Acquisition is the *mouth*; Storage is the *memory*; Analytics is the *brain*. Lose the first two and the brain starves.

---

### 1.5 Standard Units & Constants to Memorize

| Constant / Unit | Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| **1 KB** | $10^3$ bytes (decimal) | SI definition used in networking |
| **1 KiB** | $2^{10} = 1024$ bytes | Binary used in storage |
| **1 PB** | $10^{15}$ bytes | Big Data threshold |
| **HDFS Default Block Size** | **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+) | Storage chunking |
| **HDFS Default Replication Factor** | **3** | Fault tolerance |
| **Kafka Default Retention** | **7 days** | Stream buffering |
| **CAP Theorem** | Pick **2 of 3** | Brewer's conjecture |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Big Data Acquisition Pipeline (Layer-by-Layer)

Big Data acquisition is **not** a single step — it is a **4-stage pipeline**. KTU loves asking students to draw/describe this.

### Stage 1: **Data Source Identification**
- **What:** Catalog *every* possible data-emitting entity.
- **Industry 4.0 sources:**
  - **Sensors / IoT devices** → temperature, pressure, humidity, vibration, flow, level
  - **Machines / PLCs / SCADA** → Modbus, OPC-UA, MQTT telemetry
  - **Enterprise systems** → ERP (SAP), MES, CRM, SCM
  - **Web / Social** → clickstreams, tweets, reviews
  - **Multimedia** → CCTV, drone imagery, voice
  - **Transactional** → POS, billing, inventory

### Stage 2: **Data Ingestion**
The mechanism of physically pulling/pushing data from source to staging.

- **Batch Ingestion:** Data collected over a time window and uploaded periodically.
  - *Use case:* Nightly ETL of ERP sales reports.
  - *Tools:* **Sqoop, SFTP, scheduled cron jobs**.
- **Real-Time / Stream Ingestion:** Data ingested event-by-event the instant it is generated.
  - *Use case:* Live CNC spindle temperature monitoring.
  - *Tools:* **Apache Kafka, Apache Flume, AWS Kinesis, MQTT brokers**.

### Stage 3: **Data Transport**
- Movement of ingested data across the network to the storage cluster.
- Protocols: **TCP/IP, MQTT (IoT), AMQP, gRPC, HTTP/REST**.
- Concern: **network bandwidth, latency, encryption-in-transit (TLS)**.

### Stage 4: **Data Staging & Pre-processing**
- Temporary holding area where raw data is validated, deduplicated, time-stamped, and lightly transformed before permanent storage.
- Avoids polluting the main data lake with garbage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "SITS":** **S**ource → **I**ngestion → **T**ransport → **S**taging. This is the *exact* order examiners expect.

---

## 2.2 Big Data Storage Architectures

### 2.2.1 HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS is the *spine* of Big Data storage.** If you remember only one storage system, remember this.

**Architecture:** A **Master-Slave (NameNode-DataNode)** design.

| Component | Role | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **NameNode** (Master) | Stores **metadata** — filenames, directory tree, block-to-DataNode mapping | A library's card-catalog |
| **DataNode** (Slave) | Stores **actual data blocks** on local disk | The bookshelves holding the books |
| **Secondary NameNode** | Periodically merges the edit-log with the fsimage to prevent NameNode memory overflow | A librarian who periodically archives the catalog |

**HDFS Working Principle — "Write Once, Read Many":**

1. Client sends a file (e.g., 400 MB) to the NameNode.
2. NameNode splits file into **blocks of 128 MB each** → 400 MB = **4 blocks** (128 + 128 + 128 + 16).
3. Each block is **replicated 3 times** across different DataNodes (default `dfs.replication = 3`).
4. NameNode returns the list of DataNode IPs to the client.
5. Client writes block 1 directly to DataNode A, which pipelines it to B and C.

### 2.2.2 NoSQL Databases

> [!NOTE]
> **NoSQL = "Not Only SQL."** They sacrifice strict ACID transactions for **horizontal scalability** — the ability to add more cheap servers rather than buying one giant server.

| Type | Data Model | Industry 4.0 Example | Popular DB |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key-Value** | `(key → blob)` | Caching sensor readings: `sensor_42 → "23.5°C"` | **Redis, DynamoDB** |
| **Document** | JSON/BSON documents | Storing machine maintenance logs as flexible JSON | **MongoDB, CouchDB** |
| **Column-Family** | Sparse columns grouped by families | Time-series sensor data: `(timestamp, machine_id, vibration_x, vibration_y)` | **Cassandra, HBase** |
| **Graph** | Nodes + Edges | Mapping supplier-network dependencies | **Neo4j, JanusGraph** |

### 2.2.3 Data Lake vs Data Warehouse vs Data Mart

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most-asked KTU comparison.** Memorize the table:

| Feature | **Data Lake** | **Data Warehouse** | **Data Mart** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data type** | Raw (all formats) | Processed & structured | Subset of warehouse |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-read | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-write |
| **Users** | Data scientists | Business analysts | Department analysts |
| **Cost** | Low (cheap object storage) | High (specialized servers) | Medium |
| **Storage tech** | S3, HDFS, ADLS | Redshift, Snowflake, Teradata | SQL Server, Oracle |
| **Industry 4.0 fit** | Sensor + image archive | KPI dashboards | Factory-floor reports |

### 2.2.4 Cloud Object Storage

- **AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage** — store any file as an *object* with a unique URL.
- **11 nines (99.999999999%) durability** — you will lose 1 object every 10 million years.
- **Pay-per-GB-per-month** — no upfront hardware.

---

## 2.3 The CAP Theorem — Brewer's Conjecture (2000)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's #1 favourite theoretical question.** You will be asked to state, explain, or apply CAP.

In any **distributed data store**, you can simultaneously guarantee **only 2 of the 3** following properties:

| Property | Meaning | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **C — Consistency** | Every read receives the *most recent* write or an error | All nodes see the same value at the same time |
| **A — Availability** | Every request receives a (non-error) response — even if it might be stale | System stays up 24×7 |
| **P — Partition Tolerance** | System continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes | Survives server-room cable cut |

| System Type | Chooses | Example DBs |
|---|---|---|
| **CA** | Consistency + Availability (no partition tolerance — only single-server) | Traditional RDBMS: PostgreSQL, MySQL |
| **CP** | Consistency + Partition tolerance (sacrifice availability during partition) | **HBase, MongoDB (default), Redis** |
| **AP** | Availability + Partition tolerance (sacrifice strict consistency) | **Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students write *"CAP says pick 2 of 3"* — that's correct **only for distributed systems**. Since *every* real distributed system *must* tolerate partitions (network failures are inevitable), the practical choice is always **CP vs AP**, never CA. Examiners dock marks if you don't mention this nuance.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of HDFS blocks** | $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{F_{size}}{B_{size}} \right\rceil$ | $F_{size}$ = file size, $B_{size}$ = block size | blocks |
| 2 | **Total physical storage with replication** | $S_{total} = F_{size} \times R$ | $R$ = replication factor | bytes |
| 3 | **Total HDFS cluster storage** | $S_{cluster} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \times U_i$ | $D_i$ = disk of node $i$, $U_i$ = utilization | bytes |
| 4 | **Effective storage efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{F_{size}}{F_{size} \times R} = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | $R$ = replication factor | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 5 | **Ingestion throughput** | $T = \dfrac{D_{bytes}}{\Delta t}$ | $D_{bytes}$ = data ingested, $\Delta t$ = time window | bytes/sec |
| 6 | **Time-to-ingest** | $\Delta t = \dfrac{D_{bytes}}{T}$ | — | seconds |
| 7 | **Storage cost per PB/month** | $C_{PB} = C_{unit} \times 10^{6}$ | $C_{unit}$ = cost per GB | ₹ or $ |
| 8 | **Compression ratio** | $r_c = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | — | ratio (≥ 1) |
| 9 | **Data growth projection** | $D_t = D_0 \times (1 + g)^t$ | $D_0$ = current, $g$ = growth rate, $t$ = years | bytes |
| 10 | **Mean Time To Data Loss (MTTDL)** | $\text{MTTDL} \approx \dfrac{\text{MTTF}^2}{N \times N \times \text{MTTR}}$ | MTTF = mean time to failure, MTTR = mean time to repair, $N$ = nodes | hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safeguard:** All absolute-value or "such-that" bars inside tables use `\vert` instead of the raw pipe symbol `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Formulas 1 and 4 are the most-frequently tested.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains **3 full worked-out KTU-style numerical problems** with exhaustive step-by-step solutions. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 — HDFS Block Calculation & Storage Capacity

### Problem Statement
A smart factory generates a **CSV log file of size 512 MB every hour** from 200 CNC machines. This file is to be stored on HDFS with:
- Block size = **128 MB**
- Replication factor $R = 3$

**Compute:**
1. Number of HDFS blocks per file.
2. Number of blocks generated **per day**.
3. Total physical storage occupied per day on the cluster.
4. Effective storage efficiency $\eta$.
5. Project the cluster storage for **1 year** if growth rate $g = 15\%$ per year.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Number of blocks per file**

Apply formula 1 from the cheat sheet:

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{F_{size}}{B_{size}} \right\rceil
$$

Substitute $F_{size} = 512$ MB and $B_{size} = 128$ MB:

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{512 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.0 \rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}
$$

Since 512 is exactly divisible by 128, there is no partial block. Answer: **4 blocks per file**.

**Step 2 — Blocks generated per day**

Files per day = 24 hours × 1 file/hour = **24 files**.

$$
N_{daily} = 24 \times 4 = 96 \text{ blocks/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Total physical storage per day**

Apply formula 2 from the cheat sheet:

$$
S_{total} = F_{size} \times R
$$

Here we need the **raw data size per day** multiplied by replication factor:

$$
F_{daily} = 24 \times 512 \text{ MB} = 12288 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
S_{total} = 12288 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 36864 \text{ MB}
$$

Convert to GB: $36864 / 1024 = 36$ GB/day. Answer: **36 GB physical storage per day**.

**Step 4 — Effective storage efficiency**

Apply formula 4:

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333 = 33.33\%
$$

Only one-third of the cluster's *raw* disk capacity holds *useful* (non-replicated) data; the other two-thirds are duplicate copies.

**Step 5 — Projected storage after 1 year**

Apply formula 9: $D_t = D_0 \times (1 + g)^t$.

Daily storage $D_0 = 36$ GB/day. Yearly base = $36 \times 365 = 13140$ GB ≈ **12.83 TB**.

With 15% growth compounded over 1 year:

$$
D_1 = 13140 \times (1 + 0.15)^1 = 13140 \times 1.15 = 15111 \text{ GB} \approx 14.76 \text{ TB}
$$

If we project *without* growth for comparison: $14.76 - 12.83 = 1.93$ TB extra capacity needed.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Ingestion Throughput & Latency Analysis

### Problem Statement
An Industry 4.0 edge gateway aggregates data from **500 sensors** at **2 KB per reading**, every sensor generating **10 readings per second**.

**Compute:**
1. Total data ingestion rate $T$ in MB/sec.
2. Volume generated in 1 hour in MB.
3. If the network link is 100 Mbps, what is the link utilization?
4. If we apply compression with $r_c = 2.5$, what is the new link utilization?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total data rate**

Per-sensor rate = $2 \text{ KB} \times 10 \text{ readings/s} = 20 \text{ KB/s}$.

Aggregate across 500 sensors:

$$
T = 500 \times 20 \text{ KB/s} = 10000 \text{ KB/s}
$$

Convert to MB/s: $10000 / 1024 \approx 9.77$ MB/s. In Mbps: $9.77 \times 8 \approx 78.13$ Mbps.

**Step 2 — Volume in 1 hour**

Apply $\Delta t$ inversion of formula 5:

$$
D_{hour} = T \times \Delta t = 9.77 \text{ MB/s} \times 3600 \text{ s} \approx 35156 \text{ MB} \approx 34.33 \text{ GB/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Link utilization**

$$
U = \frac{T_{used}}{T_{link}} = \frac{78.13 \text{ Mbps}}{100 \text{ Mbps}} = 0.7813 = 78.13\%
$$

The link is **stressed but not saturated**. KTU rule-of-thumb: utilization > 80% warrants upgrading bandwidth.

**Step 4 — Utilization after compression**

Effective data rate after compression:

$$
T_{compressed} = \frac{T}{r_c} = \frac{9.77}{2.5} = 3.91 \text{ MB/s} \approx 31.25 \text{ Mbps}
$$

$$
U_{compressed} = \frac{31.25}{100} = 31.25\%
$$

Compression brought utilization from **78.13% down to 31.25%** — comfortable headroom.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — CAP Theorem Decision Analysis

### Problem Statement
A pharmaceutical Industry 4.0 system controls a **batch reactor** that must never dispense a wrong drug dosage. The system uses a distributed database across 3 regional plants.

**Analyze:** Which CAP trade-off (CP or AP) is appropriate? Justify with a real-world failure scenario.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant requirement**

Drug dosage = **safety-critical**. A stale (inconsistent) reading that says "10 mg dispensed" when in reality the dispensing pump just released "100 mg" could be lethal. → **Consistency is non-negotiable.**

**Step 2 — Apply CAP**

- The system is distributed (3 plants), so **partition tolerance (P) is mandatory** — network failures between plants will happen.
- Therefore the choice is between **CP** and **AP**.
- Since consistency is paramount → choose **CP**.

**Step 3 — Concrete failure scenario**

If a network cable between Plant A (dispenser) and Plant B (audit log) is cut:
- **CP system** (e.g., HBase, MongoDB with `WriteConcern Majority`) → Plant A **refuses to dispense** until connectivity is restored. The reactor halts safely. No incorrect dose is released.
- **AP system** (e.g., Cassandra) → Plant A **continues dispensing** using local data. Plants A and B may temporarily show different "total dispensed" values. When connectivity restores, the conflict must be merged. → **Unsafe** for drug dispensing.

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

> **The pharmaceutical Industry 4.0 system should be designed as a CP system**, sacrificing availability during network partitions to guarantee that all nodes always agree on the dosage record.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Storage

| Scenario | Best Storage Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| 500 TB of raw CCTV footage from a factory | **Data Lake on S3/HDFS** | Cheap, schema-on-read, write-once-read-many |
| Real-time bidding of electricity on smart grid | **In-memory Key-Value (Redis)** | Microsecond latency |
| Tracking relationships between suppliers, parts, defects | **Graph DB (Neo4j)** | Native relationship traversal |
| 10-year historical maintenance records queried by BI tool | **Data Warehouse (Snowflake)** | Structured, fast aggregations |
| Time-series vibration data from 10,000 sensors | **Column-Family (Cassandra / HBase)** | Optimized for time-stamped wide rows |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Big Data Acquisition & Storage End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[IoT Sensors]:::source
    srcB[PLC and SCADA]:::source
    srcC[ERP and MES]:::source
    srcD[CCTV and Drones]:::source

    srcA --> ingA[MQTT Broker]:::ingest
    srcB --> ingB[OPC UA Gateway]:::ingest
    srcC --> ingC[Sqoop Batch Pull]:::ingest
    srcD --> ingD[Kafka Stream]:::ingest

    ingA --> kafka[(Apache Kafka Topic)]:::stream
    ingB --> kafka
    ingC --> kafka
    ingD --> kafka

    kafka --> flink[Apache Flink Stream Processor]:::process
    kafka --> spark[Apache Spark Batch Processor]:::process

    flink --> nosqlH[NoSQL HBase Time Series]:::store
    flink --> lake[(HDFS Data Lake)]:::store
    spark --> lake
    spark --> wh[(Data Warehouse Snowflake)]:::store
    spark --> s3[(AWS S3 Object Store)]:::store

    lake --> ml[AI and ML Models]:::analytics
    wh --> bi[BI Dashboards]:::analytics
    s3 --> ml
    nosqlH --> ml

    classDef source fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef ingest fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef stream fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef analytics fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Data flows **left to right**. Sources (yellow) generate → Ingestion layer (light blue) transports → Kafka (pink) acts as the **central streaming buffer** → Processors (purple) transform → Storage tier (green) persists → Analytics (yellow) consumes. This is the *canonical* Industry 4.0 data pipeline.

---

## 4.2 HDFS Master-Slave Storage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Application]:::client --> nn[NameNode Master Holds Metadata]:::master
    nn -->|"Block locations returned"| client

    client -->|"Write Block 1"| dn1[DataNode 1]:::slave
    client -->|"Write Block 1"| dn2[DataNode 2]:::slave
    client -->|"Write Block 1"| dn3[DataNode 3]:::slave

    dn1 -.->|"Pipelines replica"| dn2
    dn2 -.->|"Pipelines replica"| dn3

    dn1 -->|"Heartbeat every 3 sec"| nn
    dn2 -->|"Heartbeat every 3 sec"| nn
    dn3 -->|"Heartbeat every 3 sec"| nn

    nn -->|"Edit log and FSImage"| snn[Secondary NameNode Checkpointer]:::master

    classDef client fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef master fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key teaching points from the diagram:**
> - The client writes **block 1 to DataNode 1**, which **pipelines** it to DataNode 2, then DataNode 3 (rack-aware replication).
> - The NameNode **never** sees the actual data — only metadata.
> - **Heartbeats every 3 seconds** — if a DataNode misses 10 consecutive heartbeats, the NameNode declares it *dead* and re-replicates its blocks.

---

## 4.3 CAP Theorem Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Designing a Distributed Data Store]:::root
    q1{Is network partition possible?}:::decision
    cpPath[Choose CP]:::cp
    apPath[Choose AP]:::ap

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|"Yes partitions can occur"| q2{Which is more critical?}
    q1 -->|"No partitions - single LAN"| caPath[CA possible - traditional RDBMS]:::ca

    q2 -->|"Absolute data accuracy must be guaranteed"| cpPath
    q2 -->|"System must remain responsive 24x7 even with stale data"| apPath

    cpPath --> cpEx[HBase, MongoDB majority, Redis cluster with replicas]
    apPath --> apEx[Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB]
    caPath --> caEx[PostgreSQL single node, MySQL single node]

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef cp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef ap fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef ca fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **This decision tree is a guaranteed 7-mark question** if drawn well in the exam. Memorize the three branches and one example DB per branch.

---

## 4.4 Data Lake vs Data Warehouse vs Data Mart — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Data All Formats]:::raw
    lake[(Data Lake)]:::lake
    wh[(Data Warehouse)]:::wh
    mart1[(Sales Data Mart)]:::mart
    mart2[(Production Data Mart)]:::mart
    mart3[(Quality Data Mart)]:::mart

    raw -->|Schema on read| lake
    lake -->|ETL transformation| wh
    wh -->|Department slice| mart1
    wh -->|Department slice| mart2
    wh -->|Department slice| mart3

    classDef raw fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef lake fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef wh fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000

```
